entries.falsifiableTrigger: rpoBacklog stops growing while leadTimes fall below 26 weeks for 2 consecutive quarters as Data Center revenue keeps rising.
entries.mechanism: NVIDIA's customers keep ordering GPUs faster than the company and its packaging suppliers can build and ship them, which keeps backlog high and lead times long.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to how quickly packaging and memory supply catch up to the order book.
entries.statement: Datacenter GPU demand continues to outrun NVIDIA's ability to serve it.
entries.title: NVDA demand durability

entries.falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS leadTimes fall below 26 weeks and HBM per S10 returns to available supply for 2 consecutive quarters while demand stays positive.
entries.mechanism: The number of accelerators NVIDIA can actually sell is set by how much advanced packaging and stacked memory its suppliers can produce, not by wafer starts or order volume.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to how fast new packaging and memory capacity from ASE, Hanmi, and others comes online.
entries.statement: Advanced packaging + HBM — not wafers or demand — cap realizable merchant-GPU revenue.
entries.title: Supply constraint binding

entries.falsifiableTrigger: AMD Data Center revenue per D2 grows below 20% YoY with no new named hyperscaler deployment for 2 consecutive quarters, or Instinct market-share-pct stalls below 6%.
entries.mechanism: AMD is turning its Instinct roadmap into signed hyperscaler deployments and fast-growing data-center revenue, which builds a real second supply option to NVIDIA.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether the OpenAI and Meta commitments convert into shipped revenue and rising share.
entries.statement: AMD converts its Instinct roadmap into material datacenter share.
entries.title: AMD credible second source

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA Data Center revenue per D2 grows above 20% YoY for 2 consecutive quarters while GPUs hold above 50% of hyperscaler build-out per market-share-pct.
entries.mechanism: When hyperscalers deploy their own chips such as Trainium and TPU, they buy fewer merchant GPUs, which would slow NVIDIA's and AMD's demand growth.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether Trainium and TPU volumes grow faster than hyperscaler merchant-GPU orders.
entries.statement: Hyperscaler custom silicon meaningfully displaces merchant-GPU demand growth.
entries.title: Custom ASIC substitution

entries.falsifiableTrigger: D6 H100 median falls below $2.50 per GPU-hour for 2 consecutive quarters, or B200 rental declines at least 15% at two or more providers within one quarter.
entries.mechanism: Cloud providers keep renting NVIDIA GPUs at high hourly rates and buyers pay above list price for B200 hardware, so added capacity has not yet forced prices down.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether the wave of new accelerator supply in the second half of 2026 outpaces rental demand.
entries.statement: GPU rental/spot price levels hold up despite added supply.
entries.title: Pricing power persistence

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Commerce removes H200 China case-by-case licensing and the 25% tariff and NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters.
entries.mechanism: U.S. export rules force AMD to license or halt China GPU shipments and add a 25% tariff, which cuts off addressable demand and triggers inventory write-downs.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to U.S. policy changes on China licensing and tariffs.
entries.statement: Export-control policy materially constrains addressable merchant-GPU demand.
entries.title: Export control exposure

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA's 10-Q shows customer financing or notes receivable rising for 2 consecutive quarters, or a named financed neocloud customer fails to convert its financed commitment to cash revenue within 1 quarter.
entries.mechanism: NVIDIA shares revenue and extends credit so AI clouds can buy its chips, which means part of reported demand rests on financing NVIDIA itself provides rather than customer cash.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to how much booked demand relies on NVIDIA's own financing versus customer cash.
entries.statement: NVIDIA's own revenue-sharing and credit-support financing increasingly underwrites its accelerator demand, coupling reported demand strength to NVIDIA-extended credit and adding circularity risk to buildout durability.
entries.title: Vendor-financed demand circularity

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA top-3 direct-customer concentration falls below 40% of total revenue for 2 consecutive quarters per customerConcentration disclosure.
entries.mechanism: A small set of large cloud buyers accounts for most accelerator revenue, so if one slows orders or shifts to in-house chips, demand drops sharply.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether NVIDIA's reach into smaller and regional buyers actually widens its revenue base.
entries.statement: NVIDIA's data-center revenue is increasingly concentrated in a few direct customers, raising demand-durability risk if any single hyperscaler slows orders or shifts to in-house silicon.
entries.title: Customer concentration risk

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA networking revenue grows slower than Data Center compute revenue for 2 consecutive quarters, or networking falls below 15% of Data Center revenue per market-share-pct.
entries.mechanism: NVIDIA sells its Spectrum-X and Quantum networking together with Blackwell GPUs, so customers who buy the compute also take the network, which raises the cost of switching to rival chips.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether customers keep buying NVIDIA networking with the GPUs rather than mixing in standalone switches.
entries.statement: NVIDIA now leads the data-center Ethernet switch market and sells that networking bundled with its GPUs, deepening a full-system moat that raises switching costs versus standalone accelerators and custom chips. NVIDIA took the top revenue spot in data-center Ethernet switching in Q1 2026 by pairing Spectrum-X and Quantum with Blackwell and GB300, and its NVLink systems cut cost per token, but no finding compares networking growth against compute growth, so the sharper claim that networking scales faster than compute is not supported.
entries.title: Networking attach as systems moat

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA cost per token fails to improve for 2 consecutive product cycles, or Data Center revenue per D2 growth turns negative for 2 consecutive quarters.
entries.mechanism: Each new NVIDIA generation does more work per chip and per watt, which lowers the cost of running each inference request and makes more workloads worth putting on its GPUs.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether each generation keeps cutting cost per token faster than rivals.
entries.statement: NVIDIA's each-generation drop in cost per token pulls more inference workloads onto its GPUs and widens the market it can serve.
entries.title: Cheaper inference pulls in demand

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Micron or its memory peers report cancellations or renegotiations of these take-or-pay agreements, or rpoBacklog tied to the deposits falls for 2 consecutive quarters.
entries.mechanism: Multi-year, non-cancelable memory contracts and large upfront deposits commit buyers to pay for accelerator supply years ahead, so much of the planned buildout is contracted rather than speculative.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to any cancellation or renegotiation of the memory take-or-pay commitments.
entries.statement: Non-cancelable multi-year HBM contracts backed by billions in customer deposits lock a large share of future accelerator demand into place and reduce the chance that today's buildout is speculative.
entries.title: Prepaid HBM take-or-pay locks in accelerator demand

entries.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters despite the tariff and volume cap.
entries.mechanism: Regulators let AMD and NVIDIA ship some China chips only under licenses, inspections, and a 25% tariff, which caps how much revenue the China channel can recover.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether Washington keeps the tariff, inspections, and volume cap in place.
entries.statement: U.S. regulators now allow case-by-case China sales of H200 and MI325X-class chips, but a 25% tariff, security conditions, and a volume cap keep any China revenue recovery small.
entries.title: Conditional China re-opening with a capped ceiling

entries.falsifiableTrigger: A funded compatibility layer or a rival software stack reaches parity such that two or more named hyperscalers move production inference off Nvidia within 2 consecutive quarters.
entries.mechanism: Rewriting AI software to run on non-NVIDIA chips is hard, so customers stay on NVIDIA even when AMD sells cheaper hardware.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to how mature AMD's ROCm software becomes for large AI workloads.
entries.statement: The difficulty of running existing AI software on non-Nvidia chips keeps customers on Nvidia even when rivals cut hardware prices.
entries.title: CUDA software lock-in sustains the moat

entries.falsifiableTrigger: AI-server memory bill-of-materials cost per S10 falls back toward baseline for 2 consecutive quarters, or DRAM output restrictions ease.
entries.mechanism: When DRAM and flash prices climb, each AI server costs more to build, which eats into system margins and can push buyers to trim orders.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to the direction of DRAM and HBM prices as new memory and packaging capacity comes online.
entries.statement: Fast-rising memory and NVMe prices are pushing up what it costs to build each AI server, which could strain buyer budgets and squeeze system margins.
entries.title: Rising memory costs inflate AI-server economics

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Combined accelerator output across NVIDIA, AMD, AWS, and Google fails to rise for 2 consecutive quarters, or packaging capacity per S10 stops expanding.
entries.mechanism: When several vendors bring next-generation chips to volume at the same time and packaging capacity grows, the total number of accelerators buyers can get rises.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether packaging and memory capacity keep pace with the multi-vendor ramp.
entries.statement: A simultaneous mass-production ramp of NVIDIA, AMD, AWS, and Google accelerators is set to expand total accelerator supply in the second half of 2026.
entries.title: Multi-vendor accelerator supply wave in second-half 2026

entries.falsifiableTrigger: Any one of the named AMD mega-deals with Meta, OpenAI, or Oracle slips its deployment schedule by more than 1 quarter, or AMD Data Center revenue per D2 growth turns negative for 2 consecutive quarters.
entries.mechanism: AMD's accelerator revenue rests on a few giant deployments, so if one partner delays or cancels, its data-center growth stalls.
entries.sensitivity: Most sensitive to the timing and follow-through of the Meta and OpenAI commitments.
entries.statement: AMD's data-center accelerator growth depends on a small set of very large commitments, which concentrates both its upside and its downside in a handful of partners.
entries.title: AMD growth leans on a few mega-deals
